food_subgroup: Leavened flatbread — stuffed and enriched naan variants

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general naan bread entry (FOODON_03302051). No specific entry exists for Kashmiri naan (dry fruit/nut-stuffed) or cheese naan variants; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers flat bread as a category. No specific code exists for stuffed or enriched naan variants; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Kashmiri naan or cheese naan as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of Kashmiri or cheese naan weighing approximately 120 g, as typically served in an Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Naan, plain, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for cheese naan and dry-fruit enrichment
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base naan macros derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (plain naan, tandoor-baked). Values adjusted upward for Kashmiri variant (dry fruits, nuts, sugar) and cheese variant (added cheese, butter) using USDA FoodData Central ingredient data. The composite represents a weighted average of the two variants as described in the restaurant menu. Energy estimated at approximately 310 kcal/100 g reflecting enriched dough with cheese and dry fruit additions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — naan bread (white wheat flour, tandoor-baked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kashmiri naan or cheese naan. GI of 71 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain white-flour naan (tandoor-baked), which is the closest analogous food. The Kashmiri variant contains added sugar and dry fruits which may slightly elevate GI; the cheese variant's fat content may modestly lower GI. A composite GI of 71 is used as a defensible estimate. GL per serving = (71 × 46.5 × 1.20) / 100 = 39.6, rounded to 39.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~504 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a naan and avoid additional salty accompaniments such as pickles or salted butter, as a full serving contributes approximately 504 mg sodium toward the 1500–2000 mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and high glycemic load per serving (~39.7)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Kashmiri or cheese naan as an occasional food only; the high GI and large glycemic load per serving can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and portion should be restricted to no more than half a piece paired with a high-fibre, low-GI accompaniment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.5 g/100 g) and refined carbohydrate content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, the saturated fat from cheese and ghee and the refined-flour base warrant moderation; choosing a smaller portion and pairing with vegetable-based dishes can help limit saturated fat and total energy intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kashmiri Naan and Cheese Naan are two enriched variants of the classic Indian tandoor-baked leavened flatbread. Kashmiri naan is made from refined wheat flour (maida) dough leavened with yeast or baking powder, enriched with yogurt and butter, and stuffed or topped with a mixture of dry fruits such as raisins and dates and nuts including cashews, almonds, and pistachios, then baked in a tandoor clay oven. The result is a slightly sweet, soft, and chewy bread with a golden-brown exterior and a rich, fragrant interior. Cheese naan follows the same dough preparation but is stuffed with paneer (Indian cottage cheese) or processed cheese and finished with butter or ghee, yielding a savory, creamy, and mildly salty flatbread. Both variants are popular in North Indian and Kashmiri cuisine and are widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, these naans are energy-dense (approximately 310 kcal per 100 g) due to refined flour, added fats, cheese, and dry fruits. They carry a high glycemic index (~71) making them a concern for blood glucose management in people with Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) is relevant for hypertension management. The saturated fat from cheese and ghee warrants moderation for cardiovascular health. They provide moderate protein (~9.2 g/100 g), calcium from dairy, and small amounts of iron and B vitamins from the wheat flour base.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kashmiri naan?
What is the difference between Kashmiri naan and plain naan?
What nuts are in Kashmiri naan?
How is naan cooked in a tandoor?

ingredient_synonyms.dry fruits: dried fruits including raisins and dates

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or USDA entry exists for Kashmiri naan or cheese naan as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain naan data with ingredient-level adjustments for dry fruits, nuts, and cheese using USDA FoodData Central. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (plain white-flour naan) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base food (plain naan) is well-characterised in approved sources and the enrichment adjustments are ingredient-grounded.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the two naan variants (Kashmiri dry-fruit/nut and cheese) as described in the Kinara – Boat Quay menu on field survey data. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for naan, and the USDA entries for naan do not consistently report vitamin D. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for naan. 4. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain white-flour naan, tandoor-baked); no direct GI measurement for Kashmiri or cheese naan was found in approved sources. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Grains and staples' as the primary component is wheat-flour flatbread; dairy (cheese) is a filling/topping. 6. The food_name has been expanded from the menu shorthand 'Kashmiri and Cheese' to the canonical English form 'Kashmiri Naan and Cheese Naan' for clarity and retrieval accuracy.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Identified that input food_group 'Dairy and eggs' was incorrect; corrected to 'Grains and staples'. Expanded food_name from menu shorthand to canonical English form. Built full nutrient profile from ICMR-NIN and USDA sources. Added GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. Populated all multilingual fields for Indian cuisine in Singapore context. Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) in notes. Confirmed no zero-macro justification needed as all core macros are non-zero.

Iteration 2: Addressed Q8 failure from previous iteration. The Hindi entry 'चीज़ नॉन' in `aliases_by_language` was inconsistent with 'चीज़ नान' in the flat `aliases` list. Corrected `aliases_by_language.hindi` to use 'चीज़ नान' to ensure verbatim consistency. Confirmed all hard gates pass and score is 8/8. Finalised as PASS.
